{{< include macros.qmd >}} :::{.r-stack} ![](Figs/nott1.png){.fragment width=100%} ![](Figs/nott2.jpg){.fragment width=100% style="transform: translateX(-90%);"} ![](Figs/nott3.jpg){.fragment width=100% style="transform: translateX(90%);"} ![](Figs/nott4.png){.fragment width=100% style="transform: translateY(-40%);"} ![](Figs/nott5.jpg){.fragment width=100% style="transform: translateY(70%);"} ::: ## University of Nottingham ::: {.columns } ::: {.column width="60%"} - Five faculties: - Arts - Engineering - Medicine and Health Sciences - Science - Social Sciences ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](Figs/nott6.jpg) ::: ::: - ~35,000 students in Nottingham, ~5,000 in Malaysia ~8,000 in China - Top 100 2026 QS World University Rankings - University Park Campus, one of the UK’s most attractive university campuses. ## School of Mathematical Sciences ::: {.columns } - ~ 80 academic staff. - Annual intake of approximately 250-300 Undergraduates ::: {.column width="50%"} Research Sections - Applied Mathematics - Pure Mathematics - Statistics and Probability - Mathematical Physics - Mathematics Education ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figs/nott7.jpg) ::: ::: ## Outline :::{=html}
Motivation for PDE-costrained Inverse Problems
Inverse Problem Framework (Ensemble Kalman Inversion).
Parameterisation for Anomaly Characterisation.
Application Examples
Future Work and Opportunities for Collaboration
::: # Motivation {#motivation background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## PDE-constrained Inverse Problems [**Aim:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Infer unknown parameters in PDE(s) given measurements of the solution of the PDE(s). ::: {.columns style="margin-bottom: 0;"} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note style="margin-bottom: 0em;"} ## Forward Problem Given $\red{\kappa(x)}$, $\red{f(x) }$ and $\red{p_0}$, find $\blue{p(x)}$ such that $$ \begin{aligned} - \nabla \!\cdot\! \big(\red{\kappa(x)}\color{black}{\nabla} \blue{p(x)} \big)&= \red{f(x) } && x\in \Omega, \\ \blue{p(x)} &= \red{p_0} &&x\in \partial\Omega . \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Given $\blue{p(x)}$, find $\red{\kappa(x)}$, $\red{f(x) }$ and $\red{p_0}$ such that $$ \begin{aligned} - \nabla \!\cdot\! \big(\red{\kappa(x)}\nabla \blue{p(x)} \big)&= \red{f(x) } && x\in \Omega, \\ \blue{p(x)} &= \red{p_0} &&x\in \partial\Omega . \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: ::: **Overarching Applications of Inverse Problems:** - Model calibration e.g. $\red{\kappa(x)}$, may be partially known (cannot be measured directly) or subject to [**uncertainty**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"}. - Tomography: inference of physical properties e.g.$\red{\kappa(x)}$ ## Application Examples {#apps-hub} :::{=html}
Composite Manufacturing
Resin Transfer Moulding.
Geophysics
Electrical Resistivity Tomography.
Built Environment
Characterisation of thermal bridges in walls.
Medical Imaging
Magnetic Resonance Elastography.
::: # Inversion Framework {#EKI background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## General framework {.scrollable} **Forward Problem (FP)**: $$ \left\{ \begin{aligned} \textit{Model Equations (MEs):} &\quad \mathcal{M}(\red{u},\blue{p}) = 0 \\ \textit{Prediction of QoI:} &\quad \blue{d} = \mathcal{H}(\blue{p}) \end{aligned} \right. $$ **Forward Map:** $\red{u}\xrightarrow{\;\;\mathcal{F}\;\;} \blue{d}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ [**Inverse Problem (IP)**:]{.alert} Find $\red{u^{\dagger}}$ (the truth) given measurements, $\color{#0044cc}{d^{\eta}}$ of $d^{\dagger}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})$. Common (modelling) assumption: $$ \begin{align*} \blue{d^{\eta}}&=\overbrace{\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})}^{d^{\dagger}}+\eta,\qquad \eta: measurement~noise \end{align*} $$ [**Classical approaches**]{.alert} Least-squares: $$ u^{\eta}=\argmin_{u\in U}~~\overbrace{ \norm{ \blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u})}^2}^{data~misfit} $$
Ill-posedness ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - Lack of uniqueness - Lack of stability - small $\eta\Rightarrow u^{\eta}\not\to u^{\dagger}$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figs/cat.png){width=80%} ::: :::
Addressing stability: Regularisation - Regularise then Optimise e.g. Tikhonov $$u^{\eta}=\argmin_{u\in U}~~\overbrace{ \norm{ \blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u})}^2}^{data~misfit}+\overbrace{\alpha\norm{ \red{u}}_{\mathcal{R}}^2}^{regularisation}$$ - Regurlise within the optimiser: - Iterarively-regularised Gauss-Newton - Landweber iteration - regularising Levenberg-Marquardt
Limitations of Regularisation: - Does not address uniqueness - Does not quantify uncertainty - Requires derivative $D\cF$ (and its adjoint $D\cF^*$) - Not straightforward to incorporate discontinuities of $\red{u(x)}$ [**Quantifying Uncertainty**:]{.alert} The Bayesian Approach - Put a prior on $\red{u}$: $\mathbb{P}(\red{u})$. - Find the posterior $$ \mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})\propto \overbrace{\mathbb{P}(\red{u})}^{prior}\overbrace{\mathbb{P}(\blue{d^{\eta}}\vert \red{u})}^{likelihood} $$ [**Challenges**:]{.alert} - In general $\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$ does not admit closed-form solution - Sampling of $\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$ (e.g. via MCMC) is computationally unfeasible. - Recall $\red{u}$ is a function (i.e. high-dimensional after discretisation). - How to incorporate discontinuities of $\red{u}$. ## Ensemble Kalman Inversion (EKI) {.scrollable} Assumption $\blue{d^{\eta}}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})+\eta,\qquad \eta\sim N(0,\Gamma)$ Hence posterior: $$ \mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})\propto \mathbb{P}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{\Gamma^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big] $$ [**Main idea:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Construct particle/sample approximations of internediate measures $$\mathbb{P}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}_{0}(\red{u})\mapsto \mathbb{P}_{1}(\red{u})\mapsto\dots \mathbb{P}_{N}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$$ ::: {.callout-tip} ## EKI Algorithm Start with samples from the prior: $\{u^{(j)}\}_{j=1}^{J}$, $u^{(j)}\sim \mathbb{P}(u)$ For $n=0,\dots, N$: - Compute $\cF^{(j)}:=\cF(u^{(j)})$ - Compute $$ \displaystyle\mathcal{C}^{\cF\cF} = \frac{1}{J-1}\sum_{j=1}^J\big(\cF^{(j)}- \bar{\cF}\big)\otimes\big(\cF^{(j)}-\bar{\cF}\big)\qquad \displaystyle\mathcal{C}^{u\cF} = \frac{1}{J-1}\sum_{j=1}^J\big(u^{(j)} - \bar{u}\big)\otimes\big(\cF^{(j)}-\bar{\cF}\big) $$ where $\displaystyle\bar{\cF} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^J \cF^{(j)}$ and $\displaystyle \bar{u} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^J u^{(j)}$\; - Update each particles, at eah iteration: $$ u^{(j)}\leftarrow u^{(j)}+C^{u\cF}(C^{\cF \cF} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}+\sqrt{\alpha_n}\xi-\cF(u^{(j)}))$$ where $\xi\sim N(0,\Gamma)$ and $\alpha_{n}$ is a regularisation/tempering parameter. ::: - [**EKI is derivative/Jacobian free**]{.bg style="--col: #e64173"}. - EKI is a heuristic approximate method - in general it does not provide samples of the exact posterior. ## Informal Derivation of EKI {.scrollable} See [@Iglesias_2021]
[**Tempering:**]{.alert} Build transitions between prior and posterior $$\mathbb{P}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}_{0}(\red{u})\mapsto \mathbb{P}_{1}(\red{u})\mapsto\dots \mathbb{P}_{N}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$$ Define $$\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{\xi_{n}}{2}\norm{\Gamma^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big]$$ with $0= \xi_{0}<\xi_{1}<\dots<\xi_{N+1}=1$. Then write recursively: $$\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{(\alpha_{n}\Gamma)^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big]$$ where $\alpha_{n}=(\xi_{n+1}-\xi_{n})^{-1}$
Linearise $\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ around the mean of $\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})$. $$\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{(\alpha_{n}\Gamma)^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(m_{n})-D\mathcal{F}(m_{n})(\red{u}-m_n))}^2 \Big]$$
Gaussianise $\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\approx N(m_{n},C_{n})$ so the linearised problem is solved in closed form for the Gaussian $\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})=N(m_{n+1}, C_{n+1})$ $$ \begin{aligned} m_{n+1}=&m_{n}+C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}(D\cF_nC_{n}\,D\cF_n^{*} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}-\cF_n)\\ C_{n+1}=&C_{n}-C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}(D\cF_nC_{n}\,D\cF_n^{*} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}D\cF_nC_{n} \end{aligned} $$ where $\cF_{n}=D\cF(m_{n})$
Approximate derivatives with covariances If $u_{n}\sim N(m_{n},C_{n})$, then $$ \begin{aligned} \E[\cF(u_{n})] \simeq &\cF_n \\ \co[\cF(u_{n})] \simeq &D\cF_nC_{n}D\cF_n^{*},\\ \co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})] \simeq & C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}. \end{aligned} $$ Hence $$ \begin{aligned} m_{n+1}\simeq &m_{n}+\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}(\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}+\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}-\red{\E_{n}[\cF(u_{n})]})\\ C_{n+1}\simeq &C_{n}-\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}(\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}+\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}\red{\co[\cF(u_{n}),u_{n}]} \end{aligned} $$
Use sample approximations of expectations/covariances $$\mathbb{P}_{n}(u_n)\simeq \mathbb{P}_{n}^{J}(u_n):=\frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\delta_{u_{n}^{(j)}},\qquad u_{n}^{(j)}\sim\mathbb{P}_{n}(u_n)=N(m_{n},\mathcal{C}_{n})$$ $$u_{n+1}^{(j)}=u_{n}^{(j)}+C_{n}^{u\cF}(C_{n}^{\cF \cF} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}+\sqrt{\alpha_n}\xi_{n}^{(j)}-\cF(u_{n}^{(j)}))$$ where $\xi_{n}^{(j)}\sim N(0,I)$ and $C_{n}^{u\cF}$ and $C_{n}^{\cF\cF}$ are the sample approximations of $\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}$ and $\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}$, respectively
## EKI history {.scrollable} - Oceanography and Meteorology: - Ensemble Kalman Filter [@Evensen;@Burgers] - (Oil) Reservoir applications: EnKF for parameter estimation. [@Naevdal;@Gu] - Adapted EnKF as an [**iterative solver**]{.alert} for PDE-constrained Inverse Problems = EKI [@IglesiasEnKF] - EKI as an **iterative regulariser**: [@EnsembleYO] - EKI as **Gaussian approximation** within a tempering scheme [@RTM;@Iglesias_2021] - EKI as a system of SDEs [@Schillings02012018] - mean-field perspective of EKI [@Calvello] # Parameterisation for Anomaly Characterisation {#level background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## Inferring Discontinuous Properties - Recall **Forward Map:** $\red{u}\xrightarrow{\;\;\mathcal{F}\;\;} \blue{d}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ - For $\red{u}$ discontinuous (or piecewise-continuous), how to define $\mathbb{P}(\red{u})$? :::{.r-stack} ![](Figs/seq3.jpg){.fragment width=70%} ![](Figs/seq2.png){.fragment width=100%} ![](Figs/seq4.png){.fragment width=40%} ![](Figs/seq1.jpg){.fragment width=100%} ::: ## Level-set random field parameterisation {.scrollable} ### Example in 2D. Unknown physical property $\kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \kappa_1(x)&\textrm{if}~x\in \Omega_{A},\\ \kappa_2(x)&\textrm{if}~x\notin \Omega_{A}.\end{array}\right.$ $\Omega_{A}$: Unknown anomalous region = [shape optiisation].{alert]. - [**Level-set parameterisation**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"}: - Introduce an artificial level-set (LS) function $L(x)$. ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - Anomalous: - $\Omega_{A}=\{x\in \mathbb{R}^{2}\vert \,\,\red{L(x)}>\lambda\}$ $$ \kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_1(x)}&\textrm{if}~\red{L(x)}>\lambda,\\ \red{\kappa_2(x)}&\textrm{if}~\red{L(x)}\leq \lambda.\end{array}\right. $$ $$\big(L, \kappa_1,\kappa_2\big)\mapsto \kappa$$ $$\mathcal{P}(L,\kappa_1,\kappa_2)=\kappa$$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} - Non-anomalous: - $D\setminus A_{L}^{\lambda}.$ ::: {layout-ncol=3} ![LS function ($L(x)$)](Figs/param1.png){height=8cm} ![Truncated LS](Figs/param2.png){height=8cm width=70%} ![Random field $\kappa(x)$](Figs/param3.png){height=8cm} ::: ::: ::: [**Bayesian level-set inversion**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} [@Level]: Instead of inferring $u(x)=\kappa(x)$, infer $u(x)=(L(x), \kappa_{1}(x),\kappa_{2}(x))$, i.e. - Prior $\mathbb{P}(u)=\mathbb{P}(L)\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1})\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{2})$ - Compute/approximate posterior: $\mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert u)$ - Push forward posterior $\mathcal{P}_\#\mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert u)$. ## Gaussian Random Fields (GRFs) {.scrollable} $L(x)\sim N(m, \mathcal{C})$
Whittle-Matern GRF (see details). Autocorrelation function: $$ ACF(x) = \sigma^2\frac{1}{2^{\nu-1}\Gamma(\nu)} \norm{\frac{x}{L}}^\nu K_\nu \Big(\norm{\frac{x}{L}}\Big). $$ $\nu$ (smoothness); $L$ (intrinsic length-scale); $\sigma$ (amplitude scale).
::: { layout-ncol=1} ![**(a)** Continuous Matérn random fields.](Figs/matern_random_fields_fft.jpg){width=100%} ![**(b)** Thresholded Matérn random fields.](Figs/matern_thresholded_fft.jpg){width=100%} ::: [**Hierarchical Bayesian level-set inversion**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} [@Dunlop]: Infer hyperparameters of random fields: ## Application Examples {#apps-hub2} :::{=html} - [[Electrical Resistivity Tomography.]{.button}](index.html#app-ert2) - Composite Manufacturing [@Deep]. - Built Environment [@IGLESIAS2024114558] - Medical Imaging [@Iglesias_2022] - Full Wave Inversion for Ground Penetraing Radar ::: ## Future Work and Opportunities for Collaboration - **Surrogate modelling via Deep Learning (Neural Operators):** build a surrogate $\mathcal{F}_s(u) \approx \mathcal{F}(u)$ to accelerate forward evaluations [@Deep]. - Composites: active structural control - Geophysics: early warning systems - Medical imaging: predictions within clinical time-scales - Thermal bridges: rapid monitoring of thermal performance - **Alternative Bayesian approaches** (e.g., MCMC) when a **full posterior characterisation** is required, particularly under **data scarcity**. - **New or existing applications** where **EKI** could provide efficient parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification. - Geometric constraints ## Thank you ### Questions? Scan for slides ![](inverse-problems-talk-qr.svg){width=35%} [[References]{.button}](index.html#ref) # Appendix ## Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) {#app-rtm} ### RTM: context ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} Manufacturing composite materials **Uses of composite materials:** Aerospace, automotive and marine industries. **Benefits:** High-performance, lightweight, flexibility to accommodate complex designs **Manufacturing Involves:** \textit{Automatic Fibre Placement:} A robotic system lays down fibre tapes into a tool. \textit{Resin Transfer Molding (RTM).} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figs/B787_composite.jpg){width=100%} ::: ::: ## RTM: Process{.scrollable} ![](Figs/RTM1.jpg){width=100%} ## Defects in RTM ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![](Figs/ICIAM2.jpg) ![](Figs/ICIAM3.jpg) ::: ## Inverse Problem in RTM [**Objective:**]{.alert} Infer physical properties of the reinforcement given pressure measurements during resin injection. [**Application:**]{.alert} Active control and Non-destructive Evaluation (NDE) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="70%"} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ::: ::: ## Resin Injection Model ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem: Find $(\blue{p,\Upsilon})$ given $\red{u}=\red{(K,\phi)}$. Darcy flow: $$ - \nabla \cdot \Big[ \frac{ \red{K(x)}}{\mu} \nabla \blue{p(x,t)} \Big] = 0,\quad \textrm{in}~ \Omega(t) \nonumber $$ Moving front: $x\in \Upsilon(t)$: $$ \frac{d \blue{\Upsilon(t)}}{dt}= - \frac{\red{K(x)}}{\red{\phi(x)} \mu} \nabla \blue{p(x,t)} \cdot \mathbf{n}(x,t) \nonumber $$ ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figures/resin_domain2.png) ::: ::: ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{(K(x),\phi(x))}$ given measurements of $\blue{p(x_{1}),\dots,p(x_{M})}$. ::: ## Goal 1: Characterisation of material properties (with uncertainty) ![](Figs/Goal1.png) ## Goal 2: Calibrated Model (for active control) Needs deep learning surrogate [@Deep] ![](Figures/Experiment4.png) ## Nottingham Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Composites Group (CG) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators" style="margin-top: 2rem;"} - *Michael Causon (SoMS)* - *Andreas Endruweit (CG)* - *Mikhail Matveev (CG)* - *Michael Tretyakov (SoMS)* ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![Advanced Manufacturing](Figs/compo_building.jpg){fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [@CAUSON2024108355; @Deep; @MATVEEV2021106323] ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![](Figs/compo.jpg) ![](Figs/MC.jpg) ![](Figs/paper_compo4.png) ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## Electrical Resistivity Tomography (ERT) {#app-ert .scrollable} ### Context and Applications - **ERT** is a non-invasive geophysical imaging method that reconstructs subsurface electrical resistivity from surface or borehole electrode measurements. - Resistivity variations reflect **soil moisture, porosity, clay content, fractures, and groundwater flow**. ![](Figs/ert_schematic.png){width=100%} **UK applications:** - **Landslides:** monitoring slope stability, detecting slip surfaces, and tracking moisture infiltration in vulnerable areas (e.g., railway cuttings, coastal cliffs). - **Flood defences:** assessing the integrity of embankments and levees, identifying internal erosion, seepage pathways, and zones of weakness. - **Other applications:** - Groundwater exploration and contamination mapping - Infrastructure and foundation assessment - Archaeology and environmental monitoring - Carbon storage and geothermal reservoir monitoring ### ERT: Forward and Inverse Problem ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem Given $\red{\kappa(x)}$ find $\blue{V(x)}$ $$\nabla \cdot \red{\kappa(x)} \color{black}{\nabla} \blue{V(x)}\color{black}{=}I(x_{s^{+}},x_{s^{-}})$$ - $\red{\kappa(x)}$: electric conductivity - $\blue{V(x)}$: electrical potential ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{\kappa(x)}$ given measurements of $\blue{V(x_{r})-V(x_{0})}$. ::: ::: ::: ## Goals {.scrollable} ### Goal 1: Infer subsurface stratigraphy and lithological under uncertainty ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Chenqui site](img/fields.jpg){width=70% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Resistivity map](img/mean.png){width=100% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Probability of zone 2](img/zone2.png){width=100% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: ### Goal 2: Anomaly Detection/Characterisation ![](Figs/ERT3D.jpg){width=100%} ## Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### SoMS (Nottigham), British Geological Survey (BGS), UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology, Lancaster University ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators" style="margin-top: 2rem;"} - *Andy Binley (Lancaster University)* - *Michael Tso (UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology)* - *Oliver Kuras (British Geological Survey)* - *Paul Wilkinson (British Geological Survey)* ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![BGS Keyworth](Figs/bgs.jpeg){width=80% fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [@Tso1; @Tso2] ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![](Figs/Andy3.jpg) ![](Figs/Andy4.jpg) ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## EKI for ERT {#app-ert2 .scrollable} ### Toy model (Synthetic data) ::: {.columns} $$ \kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_{1}}&\textrm{if}~L(x)>1,\\ \red{\kappa_2}&\textrm{if}~L(x)\leq 1.\end{array}\right. $$ ::: {.column width="50%"} Prior: $$\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1},\kappa_{2},L)=\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1})\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{2})N(0,L)$$ $$ \kappa^{(j)}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_{1}}^{(j)}&\textrm{if}~L^{(j)}(x)>1,\\ \red{\kappa_2}^{(j)}&\textrm{if}~L^{(j)}(x)\leq 1.\end{array}\right. $$ Zone 1 prob: $$\mathbb{P}(L(x)>1)\approx \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\mathbb{I}_{L^{(j)}(x)>1}$$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Posterior approx from EKI: $$\big\{k_{1}^{(j)},k_{2}^{(j)},L^{(j)}\big\}_{j=1}^{J}\sim \mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert \kappa_{1},\kappa_{2},L)$$ ![](Figs/ERT_res1.png) ::: ::: ## EKI for ERT {.scrollable} ### Extension to 3 zones with real data [@Tso1] ::: {.panel-tabset} ## Chenqui, China: :::{.columns} ::: {.column width="40%"} ![(a) Standard method. (b) EKI (mean) (c) Probability zone 2](Figs/ERT_res2.jpg){width="100%"} ![zones posterior resistivities](Figs/hist.png){width="100%"} ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} - Identify hillslope hydrostratigraphy to improve conceptualization of runoff processes in the karst catchment. - 2-D ERT profile with 48 electrodes, spaced ~5 m apart; electrode positions surveyed. - Dipole–dipole configuration with spacings of 1–3 electrodes, up to 11 levels. - Reciprocal measurements collected for error analysis (1569 measurements). - **EKI resistivity maps** better capture **weathered features and soil infills typical of karst systems**, improving geological interpretation. - **High-resistivity zone** agrees with a **flat, slightly dipping mudstone layer** reported in geological maps (Cheng et al., 2019). ::: ::: ## Eggborough, UK ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](Figs/AndyB1.jpg) ::: ::: {.column width="70%"} ![](Figs/SC.jpg){width="50%"} ![](Figs/Andy2.png) ::: ::: ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub2) ## Thermal Characterisation of Buildings' walls {#app-walls .scrollable} ### Aim and Context [**Aim:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Predict the thermal performance of (existing) buildings' walls in the presence of unknown thermal bridge effects. Context (United Kingdom) - UK decarbonisation policy legislates an 80\% reduction of its 1990 greenhouse gas emissions by 2050. - 45\% of all carbon emissions can be attributed to buildings. - 60\% of those emissions are associated space heating in domestic buildings. - 85\% of existing building stock will still be standing by 2050. - External walls comprise approximately 40\% of heat loss/gain through the building envelope ## Thermal Bridges {.scrollable} - 3D heat transfer arising from heterogeneities and/or discontinuities in the thermophysical composition of the building fabric (e.g. from material defects/degradation) ::: {.columns } ::: {.column width="60%"} - Can decrease up to 40\% the thermal efficiency of buildings - Can lead to moisture condensation. ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](Figs/seq4.png){width="75%"} ::: ::: ## Wall's thermal characterisation {.scrollable} ### Forward and Inverse Problem ::: {.columns } ::: {.column width="70%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem Given $\red{u(x)=(c(x),k(x),R_{I},R_{E})}$ find $\blue{T(x, t)}$: $$ \red{c(x)}\frac{\partial \blue{T(x, t)}}{\partial t} =\nabla \cdot \red{k(x)} \nabla \blue{T(x, t)} $$ Convective BCs on the internal/external surfaces $\mathcal{S}_{\alpha},~(\alpha=I,E)$ $$ \begin{align*} - \red{k(x)} \nabla T(x, t)\cdot\mathbf{n}(x)=\red{R_{\alpha}^{-1}}[T(x,t)- \green{T_{\alpha}(t)}] \end{align*} $$ ::: ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{u(x)=(c(x),k(x),R_{I},R_{E})}$ given $\blue{T(x,t)\big\vert_{\mathcal{S}_{\alpha}}}$ and $\blue{Q_{\alpha}(x,t)\equiv -k(x)\nabla T(x,t)\big\vert_{\mathcal{S}_{\alpha}}}$. ::: ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](Figs/wall1.png){width="60%"} ![](Figs/wall3.png){width="60%"} ::: ::: ## Nottingham Joint Work: { .scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Architecture and built environment (ABE) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators"} - Induce excitation at a given frequency (e.g. 50Hz). - Measure (complex) displacements $\blue{\bfu(\bfx)}$ via MRI (Motion encoding gradient). - Invert displacements to find shear modulus $\red{G(\bfx)}$: viscoelastic properties ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](Figs_for_MRE/Set1.png){width=95%} ::: ::: ::: {#fig-mre-workflow} ![](Figs_for_MRE/Set2.png){width=100%} A: T1/T2 weighted B: $\vert \bfu(\bfx)\vert$ C: $\vert G(\bfx)\vert\qquad$.^[Images from: Tan CH, Venkatesh SK. *MRE and Other Magnetic Resonance Imaging Techniques in Chronic Liver Disease: Current Status and Future Directions.* Gut and Liver 2016;10:672–686.] ::: ## Forward/Inverse Problem in MRE {.scrollable} ::: callout-note ## Forward Model Time-harmonic isotropic linear viscoelastic equations $$ \rho \omega^2 \blue{\bfu} +\nabla \cdot \big[ \red{G} (\nabla \blue{\bfu} +(\nabla \blue{\bfu})^{T})\big]+\nabla (\red{\lambda} \nabla \cdot \blue{\bfu})=\mathbf{0},\qquad \text{in}~ \Omega, $$ ::: - Known quantities: $\omega$ (frequency) and $\rho\approx 10^{3}\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$. - Unknown quantities: BCs, $\red{\lambda(\bfx)}$ and $\red{G(\bfx)}$ - Measured quantities (via MRI): $\blue{\bfu_{M}\equiv (\bfu(\bfx_{1}),\dots,\bfu(\bfx_{M}))}$ ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find the shear modulus $\red{G(\bfx)=G_{s}(\bfx)+iG_{l}(\bfx)}$ given measurements of $\blue{\bfu_{M}\equiv (\bfu(\bfx_{1}),\dots,\bfu(\bfx_{M}))}$. ::: $\red{G_{s}(\bfx)}$: storage modulus. $\red{G_{l}(\bfx)}$: loss modulus. ## Characterisation of biological tissue {.scrollable} ![](Figs_for_MRE/for_talk1.png){width=80% } ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/mean3.png){width=70%} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/varsto.png){width=70%} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/prob1.png){width=75%} ::: ::: ## Nottingham Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Sir Peter Mansfield Imaging Centre (SPMIC) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators"} - ::: # Motivation {#motivation background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## PDE-constrained Inverse Problems [**Aim:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Infer unknown parameters in PDE(s) given measurements of the solution of the PDE(s). ::: {.columns style="margin-bottom: 0;"} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note style="margin-bottom: 0em;"} ## Forward Problem Given $\red{\kappa(x)}$, $\red{f(x) }$ and $\red{p_0}$, find $\blue{p(x)}$ such that $$ \begin{aligned} - \nabla \!\cdot\! \big(\red{\kappa(x)}\color{black}{\nabla} \blue{p(x)} \big)&= \red{f(x) } && x\in \Omega, \\ \blue{p(x)} &= \red{p_0} &&x\in \partial\Omega . \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Given $\blue{p(x)}$, find $\red{\kappa(x)}$, $\red{f(x) }$ and $\red{p_0}$ such that $$ \begin{aligned} - \nabla \!\cdot\! \big(\red{\kappa(x)}\nabla \blue{p(x)} \big)&= \red{f(x) } && x\in \Omega, \\ \blue{p(x)} &= \red{p_0} &&x\in \partial\Omega . \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: ::: **Overarching Applications of Inverse Problems:** - Model calibration e.g. $\red{\kappa(x)}$, may be partially known (cannot be measured directly) or subject to [**uncertainty**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"}. - Tomography: inference of physical properties e.g.$\red{\kappa(x)}$ ## Application Examples {#apps-hub} :::{=html}
Composite Manufacturing
Resin Transfer Moulding.
Geophysics
Electrical Resistivity Tomography.
Built Environment
Characterisation of thermal bridges in walls.
Medical Imaging
Magnetic Resonance Elastography.
::: # Inversion Framework {#EKI background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## General framework {.scrollable} **Forward Problem (FP)**: $$ \left\{ \begin{aligned} \textit{Model Equations (MEs):} &\quad \mathcal{M}(\red{u},\blue{p}) = 0 \\ \textit{Prediction of QoI:} &\quad \blue{d} = \mathcal{H}(\blue{p}) \end{aligned} \right. $$ **Forward Map:** $\red{u}\xrightarrow{\;\;\mathcal{F}\;\;} \blue{d}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ [**Inverse Problem (IP)**:]{.alert} Find $\red{u^{\dagger}}$ (the truth) given measurements, $\color{#0044cc}{d^{\eta}}$ of $d^{\dagger}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})$. Common (modelling) assumption: $$ \begin{align*} \blue{d^{\eta}}&=\overbrace{\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})}^{d^{\dagger}}+\eta,\qquad \eta: measurement~noise \end{align*} $$ [**Classical approaches**]{.alert} Least-squares: $$ u^{\eta}=\argmin_{u\in U}~~\overbrace{ \norm{ \blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u})}^2}^{data~misfit} $$
Ill-posedness ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - Lack of uniqueness - Lack of stability - small $\eta\Rightarrow u^{\eta}\not\to u^{\dagger}$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figs/cat.png){width=80%} ::: :::
Addressing stability: Regularisation - Regularise then Optimise e.g. Tikhonov $$u^{\eta}=\argmin_{u\in U}~~\overbrace{ \norm{ \blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u})}^2}^{data~misfit}+\overbrace{\alpha\norm{ \red{u}}_{\mathcal{R}}^2}^{regularisation}$$ - Regurlise within the optimiser: - Iterarively-regularised Gauss-Newton - Landweber iteration - regularising Levenberg-Marquardt
Limitations of Regularisation: - Does not address uniqueness - Does not quantify uncertainty - Requires derivative $D\cF$ (and its adjoint $D\cF^*$) - Not straightforward to incorporate discontinuities of $\red{u(x)}$ [**Quantifying Uncertainty**:]{.alert} The Bayesian Approach - Put a prior on $\red{u}$: $\mathbb{P}(\red{u})$. - Find the posterior $$ \mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})\propto \overbrace{\mathbb{P}(\red{u})}^{prior}\overbrace{\mathbb{P}(\blue{d^{\eta}}\vert \red{u})}^{likelihood} $$ [**Challenges**:]{.alert} - In general $\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$ does not admit closed-form solution - Sampling of $\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$ (e.g. via MCMC) is computationally unfeasible. - Recall $\red{u}$ is a function (i.e. high-dimensional after discretisation). - How to incorporate discontinuities of $\red{u}$. ## Ensemble Kalman Inversion (EKI) {.scrollable} Assumption $\blue{d^{\eta}}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u^{\dagger}})+\eta,\qquad \eta\sim N(0,\Gamma)$ Hence posterior: $$ \mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})\propto \mathbb{P}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{\Gamma^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big] $$ [**Main idea:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Construct particle/sample approximations of internediate measures $$\mathbb{P}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}_{0}(\red{u})\mapsto \mathbb{P}_{1}(\red{u})\mapsto\dots \mathbb{P}_{N}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$$ ::: {.callout-tip} ## EKI Algorithm Start with samples from the prior: $\{u^{(j)}\}_{j=1}^{J}$, $u^{(j)}\sim \mathbb{P}(u)$ For $n=0,\dots, N$: - Compute $\cF^{(j)}:=\cF(u^{(j)})$ - Compute $$ \displaystyle\mathcal{C}^{\cF\cF} = \frac{1}{J-1}\sum_{j=1}^J\big(\cF^{(j)}- \bar{\cF}\big)\otimes\big(\cF^{(j)}-\bar{\cF}\big)\qquad \displaystyle\mathcal{C}^{u\cF} = \frac{1}{J-1}\sum_{j=1}^J\big(u^{(j)} - \bar{u}\big)\otimes\big(\cF^{(j)}-\bar{\cF}\big) $$ where $\displaystyle\bar{\cF} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^J \cF^{(j)}$ and $\displaystyle \bar{u} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^J u^{(j)}$\; - Update each particles, at eah iteration: $$ u^{(j)}\leftarrow u^{(j)}+C^{u\cF}(C^{\cF \cF} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}+\sqrt{\alpha_n}\xi-\cF(u^{(j)}))$$ where $\xi\sim N(0,\Gamma)$ and $\alpha_{n}$ is a regularisation/tempering parameter. ::: - [**EKI is derivative/Jacobian free**]{.bg style="--col: #e64173"}. - EKI is a heuristic approximate method - in general it does not provide samples of the exact posterior. ## Informal Derivation of EKI {.scrollable} See [@Iglesias_2021]
[**Tempering:**]{.alert} Build transitions between prior and posterior $$\mathbb{P}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}_{0}(\red{u})\mapsto \mathbb{P}_{1}(\red{u})\mapsto\dots \mathbb{P}_{N}(\red{u})=\mathbb{P}(\red{u}\vert \blue{d^{\eta}})$$ Define $$\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{\xi_{n}}{2}\norm{\Gamma^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big]$$ with $0= \xi_{0}<\xi_{1}<\dots<\xi_{N+1}=1$. Then write recursively: $$\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{(\alpha_{n}\Gamma)^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(\red{u}))}^2 \Big]$$ where $\alpha_{n}=(\xi_{n+1}-\xi_{n})^{-1}$
Linearise $\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ around the mean of $\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})$. $$\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})\propto \mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\exp\Big[-\frac{1}{2}\norm{(\alpha_{n}\Gamma)^{-1/2}(\blue{d^{\eta}}-\mathcal{F}(m_{n})-D\mathcal{F}(m_{n})(\red{u}-m_n))}^2 \Big]$$
Gaussianise $\mathbb{P}_{n}(\red{u})\approx N(m_{n},C_{n})$ so the linearised problem is solved in closed form for the Gaussian $\mathbb{P}_{n+1}(\red{u})=N(m_{n+1}, C_{n+1})$ $$ \begin{aligned} m_{n+1}=&m_{n}+C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}(D\cF_nC_{n}\,D\cF_n^{*} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}-\cF_n)\\ C_{n+1}=&C_{n}-C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}(D\cF_nC_{n}\,D\cF_n^{*} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}D\cF_nC_{n} \end{aligned} $$ where $\cF_{n}=D\cF(m_{n})$
Approximate derivatives with covariances If $u_{n}\sim N(m_{n},C_{n})$, then $$ \begin{aligned} \E[\cF(u_{n})] \simeq &\cF_n \\ \co[\cF(u_{n})] \simeq &D\cF_nC_{n}D\cF_n^{*},\\ \co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})] \simeq & C_{n}D\cF_n^{*}. \end{aligned} $$ Hence $$ \begin{aligned} m_{n+1}\simeq &m_{n}+\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}(\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}+\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}-\red{\E_{n}[\cF(u_{n})]})\\ C_{n+1}\simeq &C_{n}-\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}(\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}+\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}\red{\co[\cF(u_{n}),u_{n}]} \end{aligned} $$
Use sample approximations of expectations/covariances $$\mathbb{P}_{n}(u_n)\simeq \mathbb{P}_{n}^{J}(u_n):=\frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\delta_{u_{n}^{(j)}},\qquad u_{n}^{(j)}\sim\mathbb{P}_{n}(u_n)=N(m_{n},\mathcal{C}_{n})$$ $$u_{n+1}^{(j)}=u_{n}^{(j)}+C_{n}^{u\cF}(C_{n}^{\cF \cF} +\alpha_{n}\Gamma )^{-1}(d^{\eta}+\sqrt{\alpha_n}\xi_{n}^{(j)}-\cF(u_{n}^{(j)}))$$ where $\xi_{n}^{(j)}\sim N(0,I)$ and $C_{n}^{u\cF}$ and $C_{n}^{\cF\cF}$ are the sample approximations of $\red{\co[u_{n},\cF(u_{n})]}$ and $\red{\co[\cF(u_{n})]}$, respectively
## EKI history {.scrollable} - Oceanography and Meteorology: - Ensemble Kalman Filter [@Evensen;@Burgers] - (Oil) Reservoir applications: EnKF for parameter estimation. [@Naevdal;@Gu] - Adapted EnKF as an [**iterative solver**]{.alert} for PDE-constrained Inverse Problems = EKI [@IglesiasEnKF] - EKI as an **iterative regulariser**: [@EnsembleYO] - EKI as **Gaussian approximation** within a tempering scheme [@RTM;@Iglesias_2021] - EKI as a system of SDEs [@Schillings02012018] - mean-field perspective of EKI [@Calvello] # Parameterisation for Anomaly Characterisation {#level background-color="#2C7FB8"} ## Inferring Discontinuous Properties - Recall **Forward Map:** $\red{u}\xrightarrow{\;\;\mathcal{F}\;\;} \blue{d}=\mathcal{F}(\red{u})$ - For $\red{u}$ discontinuous (or piecewise-continuous), how to define $\mathbb{P}(\red{u})$? :::{.r-stack} ![](Figs/seq3.jpg){.fragment width=70%} ![](Figs/seq2.png){.fragment width=100%} ![](Figs/seq4.png){.fragment width=40%} ![](Figs/seq1.jpg){.fragment width=100%} ::: ## Level-set random field parameterisation {.scrollable} ### Example in 2D. Unknown physical property $\kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \kappa_1(x)&\textrm{if}~x\in \Omega_{A},\\ \kappa_2(x)&\textrm{if}~x\notin \Omega_{A}.\end{array}\right.$ $\Omega_{A}$: Unknown anomalous region = [shape optiisation].{alert]. - [**Level-set parameterisation**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"}: - Introduce an artificial level-set (LS) function $L(x)$. ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - Anomalous: - $\Omega_{A}=\{x\in \mathbb{R}^{2}\vert \,\,\red{L(x)}>\lambda\}$ $$ \kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_1(x)}&\textrm{if}~\red{L(x)}>\lambda,\\ \red{\kappa_2(x)}&\textrm{if}~\red{L(x)}\leq \lambda.\end{array}\right. $$ $$\big(L, \kappa_1,\kappa_2\big)\mapsto \kappa$$ $$\mathcal{P}(L,\kappa_1,\kappa_2)=\kappa$$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} - Non-anomalous: - $D\setminus A_{L}^{\lambda}.$ ::: {layout-ncol=3} ![LS function ($L(x)$)](Figs/param1.png){height=8cm} ![Truncated LS](Figs/param2.png){height=8cm width=70%} ![Random field $\kappa(x)$](Figs/param3.png){height=8cm} ::: ::: ::: [**Bayesian level-set inversion**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} [@Level]: Instead of inferring $u(x)=\kappa(x)$, infer $u(x)=(L(x), \kappa_{1}(x),\kappa_{2}(x))$, i.e. - Prior $\mathbb{P}(u)=\mathbb{P}(L)\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1})\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{2})$ - Compute/approximate posterior: $\mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert u)$ - Push forward posterior $\mathcal{P}_\#\mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert u)$. ## Gaussian Random Fields (GRFs) {.scrollable} $L(x)\sim N(m, \mathcal{C})$
Whittle-Matern GRF (see details). Autocorrelation function: $$ ACF(x) = \sigma^2\frac{1}{2^{\nu-1}\Gamma(\nu)} \norm{\frac{x}{L}}^\nu K_\nu \Big(\norm{\frac{x}{L}}\Big). $$ $\nu$ (smoothness); $L$ (intrinsic length-scale); $\sigma$ (amplitude scale).
::: { layout-ncol=1} ![**(a)** Continuous Matérn random fields.](Figs/matern_random_fields_fft.jpg){width=100%} ![**(b)** Thresholded Matérn random fields.](Figs/matern_thresholded_fft.jpg){width=100%} ::: [**Hierarchical Bayesian level-set inversion**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} [@Dunlop]: Infer hyperparameters of random fields: ## Application Examples {#apps-hub2} :::{=html} - [[Electrical Resistivity Tomography.]{.button}](index.html#app-ert2) - Composite Manufacturing [@Deep]. - Built Environment [@IGLESIAS2024114558] - Medical Imaging [@Iglesias_2022] - Full Wave Inversion for Ground Penetraing Radar ::: ## Future Work and Opportunities for Collaboration - **Surrogate modelling via Deep Learning (Neural Operators):** build a surrogate $\mathcal{F}_s(u) \approx \mathcal{F}(u)$ to accelerate forward evaluations [@Deep]. - Composites: active structural control - Geophysics: early warning systems - Medical imaging: predictions within clinical time-scales - Thermal bridges: rapid monitoring of thermal performance - **Alternative Bayesian approaches** (e.g., MCMC) when a **full posterior characterisation** is required, particularly under **data scarcity**. - **New or existing applications** where **EKI** could provide efficient parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification. - Geometric constraints ## Thank you ### Questions? ``` ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ::: ::: ## Resin Injection Model ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem: Find $(\blue{p,\Upsilon})$ given $\red{u}=\red{(K,\phi)}$. Darcy flow: $$ - \nabla \cdot \Big[ \frac{ \red{K(x)}}{\mu} \nabla \blue{p(x,t)} \Big] = 0,\quad \textrm{in}~ \Omega(t) \nonumber $$ Moving front: $x\in \Upsilon(t)$: $$ \frac{d \blue{\Upsilon(t)}}{dt}= - \frac{\red{K(x)}}{\red{\phi(x)} \mu} \nabla \blue{p(x,t)} \cdot \mathbf{n}(x,t) \nonumber $$ ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](Figures/resin_domain2.png) ::: ::: ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{(K(x),\phi(x))}$ given measurements of $\blue{p(x_{1}),\dots,p(x_{M})}$. ::: ## Goal 1: Characterisation of material properties (with uncertainty) ![](Figs/Goal1.png) ## Goal 2: Calibrated Model (for active control) Needs deep learning surrogate [@Deep] ![](Figures/Experiment4.png) ## Nottingham Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Composites Group (CG) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators" style="margin-top: 2rem;"} - *Michael Causon (SoMS)* - *Andreas Endruweit (CG)* - *Mikhail Matveev (CG)* - *Michael Tretyakov (SoMS)* ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![Advanced Manufacturing](Figs/compo_building.jpg){fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [@CAUSON2024108355; @Deep; @MATVEEV2021106323] ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![](Figs/compo.jpg) ![](Figs/MC.jpg) ![](Figs/paper_compo4.png) ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## Electrical Resistivity Tomography (ERT) {#app-ert .scrollable} ### Context and Applications - **ERT** is a non-invasive geophysical imaging method that reconstructs subsurface electrical resistivity from surface or borehole electrode measurements. - Resistivity variations reflect **soil moisture, porosity, clay content, fractures, and groundwater flow**. ![](Figs/ert_schematic.png){width=100%} **UK applications:** - **Landslides:** monitoring slope stability, detecting slip surfaces, and tracking moisture infiltration in vulnerable areas (e.g., railway cuttings, coastal cliffs). - **Flood defences:** assessing the integrity of embankments and levees, identifying internal erosion, seepage pathways, and zones of weakness. - **Other applications:** - Groundwater exploration and contamination mapping - Infrastructure and foundation assessment - Archaeology and environmental monitoring - Carbon storage and geothermal reservoir monitoring ### ERT: Forward and Inverse Problem ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="60%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem Given $\red{\kappa(x)}$ find $\blue{V(x)}$ $$\nabla \cdot \red{\kappa(x)} \color{black}{\nabla} \blue{V(x)}\color{black}{=}I(x_{s^{+}},x_{s^{-}})$$ - $\red{\kappa(x)}$: electric conductivity - $\blue{V(x)}$: electrical potential ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{\kappa(x)}$ given measurements of $\blue{V(x_{r})-V(x_{0})}$. ::: ::: ::: ## Goals {.scrollable} ### Goal 1: Infer subsurface stratigraphy and lithological under uncertainty ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Chenqui site](img/fields.jpg){width=70% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Resistivity map](img/mean.png){width=100% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![Probability of zone 2](img/zone2.png){width=100% fig-align="center" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: ### Goal 2: Anomaly Detection/Characterisation ![](Figs/ERT3D.jpg){width=100%} ## Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### SoMS (Nottigham), British Geological Survey (BGS), UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology, Lancaster University ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators" style="margin-top: 2rem;"} - *Andy Binley (Lancaster University)* - *Michael Tso (UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology)* - *Oliver Kuras (British Geological Survey)* - *Paul Wilkinson (British Geological Survey)* ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![BGS Keyworth](Figs/bgs.jpeg){width=80% fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [@Tso1; @Tso2] ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![](Figs/Andy3.jpg) ![](Figs/Andy4.jpg) ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## EKI for ERT {#app-ert2 .scrollable} ### Toy model (Synthetic data) ::: {.columns} $$ \kappa(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_{1}}&\textrm{if}~L(x)>1,\\ \red{\kappa_2}&\textrm{if}~L(x)\leq 1.\end{array}\right. $$ ::: {.column width="50%"} Prior: $$\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1},\kappa_{2},L)=\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{1})\mathbb{P}(\kappa_{2})N(0,L)$$ $$ \kappa^{(j)}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \red{\kappa_{1}}^{(j)}&\textrm{if}~L^{(j)}(x)>1,\\ \red{\kappa_2}^{(j)}&\textrm{if}~L^{(j)}(x)\leq 1.\end{array}\right. $$ Zone 1 prob: $$\mathbb{P}(L(x)>1)\approx \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\mathbb{I}_{L^{(j)}(x)>1}$$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Posterior approx from EKI: $$\big\{k_{1}^{(j)},k_{2}^{(j)},L^{(j)}\big\}_{j=1}^{J}\sim \mathbb{P}(d^{\eta}\vert \kappa_{1},\kappa_{2},L)$$ ![](Figs/ERT_res1.png) ::: ::: ## EKI for ERT {.scrollable} ### Extension to 3 zones with real data [@Tso1] ::: {.panel-tabset} ## Chenqui, China: :::{.columns} ::: {.column width="40%"} ![(a) Standard method. (b) EKI (mean) (c) Probability zone 2](Figs/ERT_res2.jpg){width="100%"} ![zones posterior resistivities](Figs/hist.png){width="100%"} ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} - Identify hillslope hydrostratigraphy to improve conceptualization of runoff processes in the karst catchment. - 2-D ERT profile with 48 electrodes, spaced ~5 m apart; electrode positions surveyed. - Dipole–dipole configuration with spacings of 1–3 electrodes, up to 11 levels. - Reciprocal measurements collected for error analysis (1569 measurements). - **EKI resistivity maps** better capture **weathered features and soil infills typical of karst systems**, improving geological interpretation. - **High-resistivity zone** agrees with a **flat, slightly dipping mudstone layer** reported in geological maps (Cheng et al., 2019). ::: ::: ## Eggborough, UK ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](Figs/AndyB1.jpg) ::: ::: {.column width="70%"} ![](Figs/SC.jpg){width="50%"} ![](Figs/Andy2.png) ::: ::: ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub2) ## Thermal Characterisation of Buildings' walls {#app-walls .scrollable} ### Aim and Context [**Aim:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Predict the thermal performance of (existing) buildings' walls in the presence of unknown thermal bridge effects. Context (United Kingdom) - UK decarbonisation policy legislates an 80\% reduction of its 1990 greenhouse gas emissions by 2050. - 45\% of all carbon emissions can be attributed to buildings. - 60\% of those emissions are associated space heating in domestic buildings. - 85\% of existing building stock will still be standing by 2050. - External walls comprise approximately 40\% of heat loss/gain through the building envelope ## Thermal Bridges {.scrollable} - 3D heat transfer arising from heterogeneities and/or discontinuities in the thermophysical composition of the building fabric (e.g. from material defects/degradation) ::: {.columns } ::: {.column width="60%"} - Can decrease up to 40\% the thermal efficiency of buildings - Can lead to moisture condensation. ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](Figs/seq4.png){width="75%"} ::: ::: ## Wall's thermal characterisation {.scrollable} ### Forward and Inverse Problem ::: {.columns } ::: {.column width="70%"} ::: callout-note ## Forward Problem Given $\red{u(x)=(c(x),k(x),R_{I},R_{E})}$ find $\blue{T(x, t)}$: $$ \red{c(x)}\frac{\partial \blue{T(x, t)}}{\partial t} =\nabla \cdot \red{k(x)} \nabla \blue{T(x, t)} $$ Convective BCs on the internal/external surfaces $\mathcal{S}_{\alpha},~(\alpha=I,E)$ $$ \begin{align*} - \red{k(x)} \nabla T(x, t)\cdot\mathbf{n}(x)=\red{R_{\alpha}^{-1}}[T(x,t)- \green{T_{\alpha}(t)}] \end{align*} $$ ::: ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find $\red{u(x)=(c(x),k(x),R_{I},R_{E})}$ given $\blue{T(x,t)\big\vert_{\mathcal{S}_{\alpha}}}$ and $\blue{Q_{\alpha}(x,t)\equiv -k(x)\nabla T(x,t)\big\vert_{\mathcal{S}_{\alpha}}}$. ::: ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](Figs/wall1.png){width="60%"} ![](Figs/wall3.png){width="60%"} ::: ::: ## Nottingham Joint Work: { .scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Architecture and built environment (ABE) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators"} - *Yupeng Wu (ABE)* - *Christopher Wood (ABE)* ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![ABE](Figs/abe.png){width="60%" fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [@IGLESIAS2024114558;@DESIMON2018220;@Iglesias_2018;@IGLESIAS2018417] ![](Figs/Paper_Buil.jpeg) [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## Magnetic Resonance Elastography {#app-mre .scrollable} [**Aim:**]{.fg style="--col: #e64173"} Remote palpation by shear waves, i.e. tomographic quantification of shear modulus of biological tissue. ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="60%"} - Induce excitation at a given frequency (e.g. 50Hz). - Measure (complex) displacements $\blue{\bfu(\bfx)}$ via MRI (Motion encoding gradient). - Invert displacements to find shear modulus $\red{G(\bfx)}$: viscoelastic properties ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](Figs_for_MRE/Set1.png){width=95%} ::: ::: ::: {#fig-mre-workflow} ![](Figs_for_MRE/Set2.png){width=100%} A: T1/T2 weighted B: $\vert \bfu(\bfx)\vert$ C: $\vert G(\bfx)\vert\qquad$.^[Images from: Tan CH, Venkatesh SK. *MRE and Other Magnetic Resonance Imaging Techniques in Chronic Liver Disease: Current Status and Future Directions.* Gut and Liver 2016;10:672–686.] ::: ## Forward/Inverse Problem in MRE {.scrollable} ::: callout-note ## Forward Model Time-harmonic isotropic linear viscoelastic equations $$ \rho \omega^2 \blue{\bfu} +\nabla \cdot \big[ \red{G} (\nabla \blue{\bfu} +(\nabla \blue{\bfu})^{T})\big]+\nabla (\red{\lambda} \nabla \cdot \blue{\bfu})=\mathbf{0},\qquad \text{in}~ \Omega, $$ ::: - Known quantities: $\omega$ (frequency) and $\rho\approx 10^{3}\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$. - Unknown quantities: BCs, $\red{\lambda(\bfx)}$ and $\red{G(\bfx)}$ - Measured quantities (via MRI): $\blue{\bfu_{M}\equiv (\bfu(\bfx_{1}),\dots,\bfu(\bfx_{M}))}$ ::: callout-important ## Inverse Problem Find the shear modulus $\red{G(\bfx)=G_{s}(\bfx)+iG_{l}(\bfx)}$ given measurements of $\blue{\bfu_{M}\equiv (\bfu(\bfx_{1}),\dots,\bfu(\bfx_{M}))}$. ::: $\red{G_{s}(\bfx)}$: storage modulus. $\red{G_{l}(\bfx)}$: loss modulus. ## Characterisation of biological tissue {.scrollable} ![](Figs_for_MRE/for_talk1.png){width=80% } ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/mean3.png){width=70%} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/varsto.png){width=70%} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} ![](Figs/prob1.png){width=75%} ::: ::: ## Nottingham Joint Work: {.scrollable} ### School of Mathematical Sciences (SoMS) & Sir Peter Mansfield Imaging Centre (SPMIC) ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.callout-note title="👥 Collaborators"} - *Susan Francis (SPMIC)* - *Deirdre McGrath (SPMIC)* - *Michael Tretyakov (SoMS)* ::: [@Iglesias_2022] ![](Figs/Paper.jpg){width=100%} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![SIPC](Figs/Sir-Peter-Mansfield-Imaging-Centre-SPMIC466x236.jpg){fig-align="left" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:-1em;"} ::: ::: [[Back to Applications menu]{.button}](index.html#apps-hub) ## References {#ref} ::: {#references} :::
